questions.question: Under which article can the President proclaim a National Emergency?
questions.explanation: Article 352 empowers the President to proclaim a National Emergency if the security of India is threatened by war, external aggression, or armed rebellion.

questions.question: Under which article can the President impose President's Rule in a State?
questions.explanation: Article 356 (President's Rule) is invoked when the constitutional machinery of a State fails. It is also called State Emergency.

questions.question: The President of India can be impeached for:
questions.explanation: The President can be impeached only for violation of the Constitution (Article 61). The process starts in either House (special majority of 2/3rd) and is ratified by the other House.

questions.question: The 'Kitchen Cabinet' refers to:
options: Informal group of influential advisors to PM
questions.explanation: Kitchen Cabinet is an informal term for a small group of close advisors (not necessarily ministers) who the PM consults informally on important decisions, bypassing the formal Cabinet.

questions.question: The 'Union Cabinet' is different from 'Council of Ministers' in that:
options: Union Cabinet is a smaller body of senior Cabinet Ministers
Council of Ministers has only PM and senior ministers
questions.explanation: Council of Ministers includes Cabinet Ministers, Ministers of State (independent charge), and Ministers of State (no independent charge). Cabinet is a smaller sub-set of senior ministers who collectively decide policy.

questions.question: The Governor's rule in a state (Article 356) was first imposed in:
questions.explanation: Article 356 (President's Rule) was first imposed in Punjab in 1951. It has been imposed over 100 times since independence — Rajasthan and Uttar Pradesh have had it most often.

questions.question: The Advocate General of a State is appointed by:
questions.explanation: Article 165: Advocate General is appointed by the Governor. The Advocate General is the chief law officer of the state, analogous to the Attorney General of India at the Union level.

questions.question: The Attorney General of India is appointed by:
questions.explanation: Article 76: Attorney General is appointed by the President (on advice of the Council of Ministers). The AG must be qualified to be a judge of the Supreme Court.

questions.question: The Central Information Commission (CIC) was established under:
questions.explanation: Central Information Commission was established under the Right to Information Act 2005 to hear appeals and complaints related to public information. Chief Information Commissioner and commissioners are appointed by President.

questions.question: The Inter-State Council is chaired by:
questions.explanation: The Inter-State Council (Article 263) is chaired by the Prime Minister. It consists of Chief Ministers of all states and 6 Cabinet Ministers of the Union as members.

questions.question: Who administers the oath of office to the Vice President of India?
questions.explanation: The President administers the oath of office to the Vice President (Art 69). Whereas, the Chief Justice of India administers the oath to the President.

questions.question: Under which Article is the Prime Minister appointed?
questions.explanation: Article 75(1) states the Prime Minister shall be appointed by the President. Article 74 states the Council of Ministers with the PM at head shall aid and advise the President.

questions.question: Which case established that the President must act on the advice of the Council of Ministers?
options: Shamsher Singh v. State of Punjab (1974)
questions.explanation: Shamsher Singh v. State of Punjab (1974) — the Supreme Court held that the President and Governors are constitutional heads and must act on the advice of the Cabinet/Council of Ministers.

questions.question: A Judge of a High Court can be transferred by:
options: President on recommendation of CJI after consultation with collegium
questions.explanation: A Judge of a High Court can be transferred by the President after consultation with the Chief Justice of India (Art 222). The collegium system now governs such transfers.

questions.question: The Governor of a State holds office:
options: During the pleasure of the President
For life tenure like a judge
questions.explanation: The Governor holds office during the pleasure of the President (Art 156). Though the normal term is 5 years, the President (on advice of Cabinet) can remove the Governor at any time.

questions.question: Under Article 163, the Governor can act in his discretion regarding:
options: Certain specific matters listed in the Constitution
None — Governor must always act on CM's advice
questions.explanation: Article 163 states Governor acts on the advice of the Council of Ministers except when the Constitution expressly requires him to act in his discretion (like sending report for President's Rule under Art 356).

questions.question: Who appoints the Advocate General of a State?
questions.explanation: The Advocate General is appointed by the Governor (Art 165). He/she must be qualified to be a judge of the High Court and holds office during the pleasure of the Governor.

questions.question: The Finance Commission of India is constituted under:
questions.explanation: The Finance Commission is constituted under Article 280 by the President every fifth year (or earlier). It recommends distribution of taxes between Centre and States and grants-in-aid to States.

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Pleasure' in the Indian Constitution means:
options: Certain officers hold office at the pleasure of the President/Governor
Citizens have the pleasure of removing officials
Courts can remove officials at pleasure
Parliament can dismiss officers at any time
questions.explanation: The Doctrine of Pleasure (Art 310) means certain government servants hold office during the pleasure of the President or Governor. However, Art 311 protects civil servants from arbitrary dismissal — they must be given a hearing.

questions.question: Which Article provides for special provisions relating to language spoken by a section of a State's population?
questions.explanation: Article 347 states the President may, on demand, direct that a language spoken by a substantial proportion of the State's population be officially recognized for that State. Punjabi in Haryana is an example.

questions.question: Under Article 72, the President's pardoning power includes:
options: All except offences under State laws
questions.explanation: Article 72 gives the President power to grant pardon, reprieve, respite, remission of punishment or commute the sentence — in cases of court martial, offences against Union laws, and death sentences.

questions.question: The power of the Governor to pardon under Article 161 differs from Art 72 (President) in that the Governor:
options: Can pardon all types of offences
Has more powers than the President
questions.explanation: Under Art 161, the Governor can pardon, reprieve, respite, remit, or commute sentences for offences against State laws. But unlike the President, the Governor CANNOT pardon death sentences — only the President can under Art 72.

questions.question: The Constitution of Jammu & Kashmir was abrogated by which action?
options: Presidential Order abrogating Article 370 in August 2019
J&K Reorganization Act 2019 replacing the State with two UTs
Both Presidential Order (abrogating Art 370) and J&K Reorganization Act 2019
questions.explanation: Article 370 was abrogated by Presidential Order on 5 August 2019, and simultaneously the J&K Reorganization Act 2019 bifurcated the State into two Union Territories — J&K (with legislature) and Ladakh (without legislature).

questions.question: The Constitutional provision for special officer for Linguistic Minorities is under:
questions.explanation: Article 350B provides for a Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities to investigate all matters relating to safeguards for linguistic minorities. The officer reports to the President annually.

questions.question: The National Development Council (NDC) is chaired by:
questions.explanation: The National Development Council is chaired by the Prime Minister. It includes all Union Cabinet Ministers, Chief Ministers of all States, Lt. Governors/Administrators of UTs, and members of NITI Aayog.

questions.question: Which Article deals with the composition of the UPSC?
questions.explanation: Article 316 deals with the appointment and term of office of UPSC members. The Chairman and members are appointed by the President. They can serve for 6 years or until age 65, whichever is earlier.

questions.question: Which article gives the President the power to grant pardon?
questions.explanation: Article 72 empowers the President to grant pardon, reprieve, respite, or remission of punishment to persons convicted of offences.

questions.question: Article 356 (President's Rule) was interpreted in which landmark case to require 'objective satisfaction'?
options: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994)
questions.explanation: In S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994), the Supreme Court held that imposition of President's Rule is subject to judicial review and the President must have 'objective satisfaction' based on relevant material.

questions.question: The Finance Commission of India is constituted by:
options: President of India every 5 years (Article 280) to recommend how taxes are divided between Centre and States
questions.explanation: The Finance Commission (Article 280) is constituted by the President every 5 years. It recommends: how central taxes are shared with states, grants-in-aid to states, and any other matter referred. The 15th Finance Commission (2021-26) recommended 41% tax devolution to states.

questions.question: Who administers the oath to the President?
questions.explanation: The Chief Justice of India administers the oath to the President (Article 60). In CJI's absence, the senior-most judge of the Supreme Court administers it.

questions.question: The Governor of a state is appointed by:
questions.explanation: The Governor is appointed by the President of India on the advice of the Central Government (Article 155). The Governor is the constitutional head of the state.

questions.question: Chief Minister is appointed by:
questions.explanation: Chief Minister is appointed by the Governor. Conventionally, the leader of the majority party/coalition in the State Legislative Assembly is appointed CM. CM need not be a sitting MLA.

questions.question: The Finance Commission is constituted under:
questions.explanation: Article 280: Finance Commission is constituted by the President every 5 years to recommend distribution of tax revenues between Centre and States and grants-in-aid to states.

questions.question: The Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) is established by:
questions.explanation: Article 315 establishes the UPSC. It conducts examinations for appointments to the services of the Union. Its chairman is appointed by the President.

questions.question: The Chairman of NITI Aayog is:
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister is the ex-officio Chairman of NITI Aayog. It also has a CEO (appointed by PM), Vice Chairman, and Governing Council (all CMs and LGs of states/UTs).

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution talks about abolition of titles?
questions.explanation: Article 18 abolishes titles: No title shall be conferred by the State. Citizens cannot accept titles from foreign governments without President's consent. Exceptions: Military/academic distinctions.

questions.question: The Inter-State Council (Art 263) is chaired by:
questions.explanation: The Inter-State Council is chaired by the Prime Minister and includes Chief Ministers of all States and Administrators of UTs with legislatures, plus 6 Cabinet Ministers nominated by PM.

questions.question: The Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) is a:
questions.explanation: UPSC is a Constitutional body established under Article 315. It recruits civil servants for All India Services and Central Services. Its Chairperson can be removed only by Presidential order.

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Pleasure' in the Indian Constitution is modified in the case of:
options: Judges of High Courts and Supreme Court
questions.explanation: Article 310 provides that government servants hold office 'at the pleasure of the President.' However, this is modified for judges of Supreme Court and High Courts, CAG, UPSC members — they can only be removed through impeachment-like processes.

questions.question: Union Territories are administered by:
questions.explanation: Union Territories are administered by the President of India (Article 239) through a Lieutenant Governor (LG) or Administrator appointed by the President.

questions.question: The President of India is elected by an electoral college consisting of members of both Houses of Parliament and the Legislative Assemblies of the states. The election is held in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of a single transferable vote. The President must be a citizen of India, at least 35 years old, and eligible to be a member of the Lok Sabha.
questions.explanation: The election of the President is governed by Article 55, while Article 56 deals with the term of office of the President.

questions.question: The Council of Ministers is headed by the Prime Minister, who is appointed by the President. The Prime Minister is responsible for allocating portfolios to the Ministers and advising the President on various matters. The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.
questions.explanation: Article 74 deals with the Council of Ministers, while Article 75 deals with the appointment of the Prime Minister and other Ministers.

questions.question: The Governor of a state is appointed by the President and holds office during the pleasure of the President. The Governor has the power to appoint the Chief Minister and other Ministers, and also has the power to summon and dissolve the Legislative Assembly.
questions.explanation: Article 155 deals with the appointment of the Governor, while Article 156 deals with the term of office of the Governor.

questions.question: The Supreme Court has the power to declare a law passed by Parliament or a state legislature as unconstitutional if it violates the provisions of the Constitution. The Supreme Court also has the power to issue writs for the enforcement of fundamental rights.
questions.explanation: Article 13 deals with the power of the Supreme Court to declare laws as unconstitutional, while Article 32 deals with the power of the Supreme Court to issue writs for the enforcement of fundamental rights.

questions.question: The President has the power to grant pardons, reprieves, and remissions of punishment to persons convicted of offenses. The President also has the power to commute sentences and suspend sentences.
questions.explanation: Article 72 deals with the power of the President to grant pardons, reprieves, and remissions of punishment.

questions.question: The Prime Minister is responsible for communicating to the President all decisions of the Council of Ministers. The Prime Minister is also responsible for furnishing information to the President on any matter that the President may require.
questions.explanation: Article 78 deals with the duty of the Prime Minister to communicate decisions of the Council of Ministers to the President.

questions.question: The Governor of a state has the power to reserve a bill passed by the Legislative Assembly for the consideration of the President. The Governor also has the power to recommend the imposition of President's rule in the state.
questions.explanation: Article 200 deals with the power of the Governor to reserve a bill for the consideration of the President.

questions.question: The President has the power to appoint the Attorney General of India, who is the chief legal advisor to the Government of India. The President also has the power to appoint the Comptroller and Auditor General of India.
questions.explanation: Article 76 deals with the appointment of the Attorney General, while Article 77 deals with the appointment of the Comptroller and Auditor General.

questions.question: The Council of Ministers is responsible for aiding and advising the President in the exercise of his functions. The Council of Ministers is also responsible for formulating policies and implementing programs of the government.
questions.explanation: Article 74 deals with the Council of Ministers, while Article 75 deals with the appointment of the Prime Minister and other Ministers.

questions.question: The Supreme Court has the power to adjudicate disputes between the Centre and states, and between states. The Supreme Court also has the power to interpret the Constitution and declare laws as unconstitutional.
questions.explanation: Article 131 deals with the original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court to adjudicate disputes between the Centre and states.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President of India is the head of the executive. Statement 2: The President has the power to appoint the Prime Minister.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 53 and 75 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Council of Ministers is headed by the Prime Minister. Statement 2: The Prime Minister is appointed by the President.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, the Council of Ministers is headed by the Prime Minister who is appointed by the President.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the executive?
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister is the head of the government.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President has the power to dissolve the Lok Sabha. Statement 2: The President can appoint a Prime Minister without the support of the majority in the Lok Sabha.
questions.explanation: The President can dissolve the Lok Sabha on the advice of the Prime Minister.

questions.question: Which article deals with the executive power of the Union?
questions.explanation: Article 53 deals with the executive power of the Union.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power of judicial review. Statement 2: The Supreme Court can declare a law passed by the Parliament as unconstitutional.
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has the power of judicial review and can declare a law as unconstitutional.

questions.question: Which of the following is a feature of the Indian executive?
questions.explanation: The Indian executive is parliamentary in nature.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Prime Minister is responsible to the Lok Sabha. Statement 2: The Prime Minister can be removed by a no-confidence motion.
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister is responsible to the Lok Sabha and can be removed by a no-confidence motion.

questions.question: Which article deals with the appointment of the Prime Minister?
questions.explanation: Article 75 deals with the appointment of the Prime Minister.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President has the power to grant pardon. Statement 2: The President can appoint the Chief Justice of India.
questions.explanation: The President has the power to grant pardon and appoint the Chief Justice of India.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Indian President: 1. The President is elected by an electoral college consisting of elected members of both Houses of Parliament and the Legislative Assemblies of the states. 2. The President has the power to appoint the Prime Minister, but this appointment is subject to the support of the majority in the Lok Sabha.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Articles 55 and 75 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Council of Ministers: 1. The Council of Ministers is headed by the Prime Minister. 2. The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 75 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Governor of a state: 1. The Governor is appointed by the President. 2. The Governor has the power to appoint the Chief Minister of the state.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Articles 155 and 164 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Chief Minister: 1. The Chief Minister is the head of the government in a state. 2. The Chief Minister is responsible for advising the Governor on the appointment of ministers.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per the Indian Constitution and conventions.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Prime Minister: 1. The Prime Minister is the head of the government in India. 2. The Prime Minister has the power to advise the President on the appointment of ministers.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 75 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Cabinet Secretariat: 1. The Cabinet Secretariat is responsible for coordinating the work of the ministries. 2. The Cabinet Secretariat is headed by the Cabinet Secretary.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per the Indian government's organizational structure.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Attorney General of India: 1. The Attorney General is the highest law officer in the country. 2. The Attorney General has the right to speak in both Houses of Parliament.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 76 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Comptroller and Auditor General of India: 1. The Comptroller and Auditor General is responsible for auditing the accounts of the government. 2. The Comptroller and Auditor General has the power to investigate the expenditure of the government.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 148 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Union Public Service Commission: 1. The Union Public Service Commission is responsible for conducting examinations for recruitment to the civil services. 2. The Union Public Service Commission has the power to advise the President on matters related to the civil services.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 320 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the President's rule: 1. The President's rule can be imposed in a state if the government of the state cannot be carried on in accordance with the provisions of the Constitution. 2. The President's rule is imposed under Article 356 of the Indian Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 356 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Indian President: 1. The President is elected by an electoral college consisting of the elected members of both Houses of Parliament and the elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of the States. 2. The President holds office for a term of five years from the date on which he enters upon his office.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 55 and Article 56 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Indian Prime Minister: 1. The Prime Minister is appointed by the President. 2. The Prime Minister must be a member of the Lok Sabha.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 75 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Council of Ministers: 1. The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha. 2. The Council of Ministers is headed by the Prime Minister.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 75 and Article 78 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Governor of a State: 1. The Governor is appointed by the President. 2. The Governor holds office for a term of five years from the date on which he enters upon his office.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 155 and Article 156 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Chief Minister of a State: 1. The Chief Minister is appointed by the Governor. 2. The Chief Minister must be a member of the State Legislative Assembly.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 164 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Attorney General of India: 1. The Attorney General is appointed by the President. 2. The Attorney General must be a qualified lawyer.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 76 and Article 77 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Comptroller and Auditor General of India: 1. The Comptroller and Auditor General is appointed by the President. 2. The Comptroller and Auditor General is responsible for auditing the accounts of the Union and the States.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 148 and Article 149 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Union Territories: 1. The Union Territories are administered by the President. 2. The Union Territories have no Legislative Assemblies.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 239 and Article 240 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the National Commission to Review the Working of the Constitution: 1. The Commission was established in 2000. 2. The Commission was headed by Justice M.N. Venkatachaliah.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per the terms of reference of the Commission.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Supreme Court's judgment in the Kesavananda Bharati case: 1. The judgment was delivered in 1973. 2. The judgment held that the Parliament has the power to amend the Constitution but not to alter its basic structure.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per the judgment of the Supreme Court in the Kesavananda Bharati case.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Council of Ministers is headed by the Prime Minister. Statement 2: The Council of Ministers is responsible for aiding and advising the President.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true as per Article 74 and 75 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are true about the Indian executive? Statement 1: The President has the power to grant pardons. Statement 2: The Governor has the power to appoint the Chief Minister.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true as per Article 72 and 164 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Prime Minister is the head of the government. Statement 2: The President is the head of the state.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true as per the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are true about the Indian executive? Statement 1: The President is elected by the electoral college. Statement 2: The Vice-President is elected by the Lok Sabha.
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is true as per Article 55 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Governor has the power to appoint the Chief Minister. Statement 2: The Chief Minister is the head of the state government.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true as per Article 164 and 167 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are true about the Indian executive? Statement 1: The President has the power to dissolve the Lok Sabha. Statement 2: The Governor has the power to dissolve the state assembly.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true as per Article 85 and 356 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha. Statement 2: The Council of Ministers is responsible for aiding and advising the President.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true as per Article 75 and 74 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are true about the Indian executive? Statement 1: The President is the supreme commander of the armed forces. Statement 2: The Prime Minister is the head of the government.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true as per Article 53 and the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Governor has the power to appoint the Advocate General. Statement 2: The President has the power to appoint the Attorney General.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true as per Article 165 and 76 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: The President of India can appoint the Prime Minister without consulting anyone as per Article 75, but they typically choose the leader of the majority party. The President also has the power to appoint Governors of states. Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 75 pertains to the Council of Ministers and Article 155 to the appointment of Governors.

questions.question: The Council of Ministers is headed by the Prime Minister, who is appointed by the President. The Ministers are collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha. Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 74 deals with the Council of Ministers and Article 75 with its appointment and responsibilities.

questions.question: The President of India has the power to grant pardons, reprieves, and respites. This power is exercised in accordance with Article 72. Which of the following is also a correct statement about the President's powers?
questions.explanation: The President's powers include executive powers as the head of the executive.

questions.question: The Prime Minister of India communicates the decisions of the Council of Ministers to the President. The Prime Minister is also the leader of the Lok Sabha. Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 78 pertains to the communication of decisions and Article 75 to the appointment of the Prime Minister.

questions.question: The Governor of a state in India has the power to appoint the Chief Minister. The Governor also has the power to reserve a bill for the President's consideration. Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 163 deals with the Governor's powers and Article 200 with the reservation of bills.

questions.question: The Chief Minister of a state is appointed by the Governor. The Chief Minister advises the Governor on the appointment of other Ministers. Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 164 pertains to the appointment of the Chief Minister and Article 167 to their duties.

questions.question: The President of India can dissolve the Lok Sabha on the advice of the Prime Minister. The President also has the power to summon and prorogue the Lok Sabha. Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 85 deals with the summoning and prorogation of the Lok Sabha and Article 87 with its dissolution.

questions.question: The Council of Ministers is responsible for aiding and advising the President. The Prime Minister is the head of the Council of Ministers. Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 74 deals with the Council of Ministers and Article 75 with its appointment and responsibilities.

questions.question: The Governor of a state has the power to appoint the Advocate General. The Governor also has the power to make rules for the convenient transaction of business. Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 165 deals with the appointment of the Advocate General and Article 166 with the transaction of business.

questions.question: The President of India has the power to appoint the Chairman and members of the Union Public Service Commission. The President also has the power to remove them. Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 316 deals with the appointment and Article 317 with the removal of the Chairman and members.

questions.question: The President of India is elected by an electoral college consisting of members of both Houses of Parliament and the Legislative Assemblies of the states. Statement 1: The election is held in accordance with the system of proportional representation. Statement 2: The President has the power to appoint the Prime Minister.
questions.explanation: Article 55 of the Constitution provides for the election of the President by an electoral college, and Article 75 provides for the appointment of the Prime Minister.

questions.question: The Council of Ministers is headed by the Prime Minister, who is appointed by the President. Statement 1: The Prime Minister is responsible for advising the President. Statement 2: The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.
questions.explanation: Article 74 provides for the Prime Minister's role in advising the President, and Article 75 provides for collective responsibility to the Lok Sabha.

questions.question: The Governor of a state is appointed by the President and has the power to appoint the Chief Minister. Statement 1: The Governor has the power to dissolve the Legislative Assembly. Statement 2: The Chief Minister is responsible for advising the Governor.
questions.explanation: Article 163 provides for the Governor's power to appoint the Chief Minister, and Article 164 provides for the Chief Minister's responsibility to advise the Governor.

questions.question: The Supreme Court has the power to adjudicate on disputes relating to the election of the President and the Vice-President. Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power to declare a law passed by Parliament as unconstitutional. Statement 2: The President has the power to appoint judges to the Supreme Court.
questions.explanation: Article 71 provides for the Supreme Court's power to adjudicate on presidential and vice-presidential election disputes, and Article 124 provides for the President's power to appoint judges.

questions.question: The President has the power to grant pardons, reprieves, and respites to persons convicted of offences. Statement 1: The President's power to grant pardons is absolute. Statement 2: The President's power to grant pardons is subject to the advice of the Prime Minister.
questions.explanation: Article 72 provides for the President's power to grant pardons, which is subject to the advice of the Prime Minister.

questions.question: The Prime Minister is responsible for communicating the decisions of the Council of Ministers to the President. Statement 1: The Prime Minister is responsible for advising the President on the appointment of judges to the Supreme Court. Statement 2: The Prime Minister is responsible for advising the President on the grant of pardons.
questions.explanation: Article 78 provides for the Prime Minister's responsibility to communicate the decisions of the Council of Ministers to the President.

questions.question: The Governor of a state has the power to reserve a bill passed by the Legislative Assembly for the consideration of the President. Statement 1: The Governor has the power to dissolve the Legislative Assembly. Statement 2: The Governor has the power to appoint the Chief Minister.
questions.explanation: Article 200 provides for the Governor's power to reserve a bill for the President's consideration, and Article 164 provides for the Governor's power to appoint the Chief Minister.

questions.question: The President has the power to summon, prorogue, and dissolve the Lok Sabha. Statement 1: The President's power to dissolve the Lok Sabha is absolute. Statement 2: The President's power to dissolve the Lok Sabha is subject to the advice of the Prime Minister.
questions.explanation: Article 85 provides for the President's power to summon, prorogue, and dissolve the Lok Sabha, which is subject to the advice of the Prime Minister.

questions.question: The Chief Minister of a state is responsible for advising the Governor on the appointment of ministers. Statement 1: The Chief Minister is responsible for communicating the decisions of the Council of Ministers to the Governor. Statement 2: The Chief Minister is responsible for advising the Governor on the grant of pardons.
questions.explanation: Article 167 provides for the Chief Minister's responsibility to communicate the decisions of the Council of Ministers to the Governor.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President of India is the head of the executive. Statement 2: The President has the power to appoint the Prime Minister. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 53 and 75 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Council of Ministers is headed by the Prime Minister. Statement 2: The Prime Minister is appointed by the President. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 75 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Governor of a state has the power to appoint the Chief Minister. Statement 2: The Chief Minister is the head of the state government. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 164 and 167 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President can dissolve the Lok Sabha on the advice of the Prime Minister. Statement 2: The President has the power to appoint judges to the Supreme Court. Which of the statements is/are correct?
